core_meaning: <ruby>入<rt>い</rt></ruby>れる means 'to put something into / inside something else' — it's the transitive counterpart of <ruby>入<rt>はい</rt></ruby>る (which means 'to enter' on its own). Beyond physical insertion, it extends to brewing drinks, including someone in a group, pressing a switch, and making a reservation or appointment.

sentences.jp: <ruby>鍵<rt>かぎ</rt></ruby>、バッグに<ruby>入<rt>い</rt></ruby>れといたよ。
sentences.translation: I put your keys in the bag.
sentences.notes: Core physical sense of putting something inside a container; 〜ておいた contracts to 〜といた, which is how it actually sounds in casual speech.

sentences.jp: コーヒーに<ruby>砂糖<rt>さとう</rt></ruby>を<ruby>入<rt>い</rt></ruby>れますか？
sentences.translation: Do you take sugar in your coffee?
sentences.notes: <ruby>入<rt>い</rt></ruby>れる is the natural verb for adding ingredients to a drink or dish — 'adding sugar/milk' is always <ruby>入<rt>い</rt></ruby>れる, not <ruby>置<rt>お</rt></ruby>く or <ruby>加<rt>くわ</rt></ruby>える in everyday speech.

sentences.jp: <ruby>来週<rt>らいしゅう</rt></ruby>の<ruby>会議<rt>かいぎ</rt></ruby>に<ruby>田中<rt>たなか</rt></ruby>さんも<ruby>入<rt>い</rt></ruby>れてもらえますか？
sentences.translation: Could you include Tanaka-san in next week's meeting too?
sentences.notes: Shows the 'include a person' sense — <ruby>入<rt>い</rt></ruby>れる stretches naturally to adding someone to a group, meeting, or list.

sentences.jp: <ruby>洗濯機<rt>せんたくき</rt></ruby>のスイッチを<ruby>入<rt>い</rt></ruby>れてから<ruby>出<rt>で</rt></ruby>かけた。
sentences.translation: I turned on the washing machine before heading out.
sentences.notes: スイッチを<ruby>入<rt>い</rt></ruby>れる is the fixed expression for switching a device on; the opposite is スイッチを<ruby>切<rt>き</rt></ruby>る.

sentences.jp: このチームには<ruby>経験者<rt>けいけんしゃ</rt></ruby>を<ruby>入<rt>い</rt></ruby>れるべきだと<ruby>考<rt>かんが</rt></ruby>えております。
sentences.translation: We believe experienced personnel should be brought into this team.
sentences.notes: In formal/business contexts <ruby>入<rt>い</rt></ruby>れる is used for bringing people or elements into an organization or plan, often carrying a deliberate, strategic nuance.

dont_use: <ruby>入<rt>い</rt></ruby>れる requires a direct object — something is being put IN somewhere. Don't confuse it with <ruby>入<rt>はい</rt></ruby>る, which is intransitive ('something goes in by itself'). For example, you can't say ×<ruby>私<rt>わたし</rt></ruby>は<ruby>部屋<rt>へや</rt></ruby>に<ruby>入<rt>い</rt></ruby>れた to mean 'I entered the room' — that's <ruby>部屋<rt>へや</rt></ruby>に<ruby>入<rt>はい</rt></ruby>った. Also avoid using <ruby>入<rt>い</rt></ruby>れる where <ruby>加<rt>くわ</rt></ruby>える or <ruby>追加<rt>ついか</rt></ruby>する is more appropriate in formal written contexts, such as adding a clause to a contract.

confused_with.contrast: A very common mistake is using <ruby>入<rt>い</rt></ruby>れる when you mean 'to go/come into' a place — for instance, saying ×<ruby>風呂<rt>ふろ</rt></ruby>に<ruby>入<rt>い</rt></ruby>れた instead of ○<ruby>風呂<rt>ふろ</rt></ruby>に<ruby>入<rt>はい</rt></ruby>った ('I got in the bath'). <ruby>入<rt>はい</rt></ruby>る is intransitive — the subject moves in on their own — while <ruby>入<rt>い</rt></ruby>れる is transitive and always needs something being put inside something else. When YOU go somewhere, always use <ruby>入<rt>はい</rt></ruby>る; when you PUT something somewhere, use <ruby>入<rt>い</rt></ruby>れる.

confusion_set.use_when: YOU are putting/placing something inside something else (transitive)
confusion_set.example: <ruby>財布<rt>さいふ</rt></ruby>をポケットに<ruby>入<rt>い</rt></ruby>れた。(I put my wallet in my pocket.)

confusion_set.use_when: The subject moves into a place or state on their own (intransitive)
confusion_set.example: <ruby>彼<rt>かれ</rt></ruby>は<ruby>部屋<rt>へや</rt></ruby>に<ruby>入<rt>はい</rt></ruby>った。(He entered the room.)

confusion_set.use_when: You make or let someone ELSE put something in (causative of <ruby>入<rt>い</rt></ruby>れる)
confusion_set.example: <ruby>子供<rt>こども</rt></ruby>に<ruby>荷物<rt>にもつ</rt></ruby>を<ruby>入<rt>い</rt></ruby>れさせた。(I had the child put the luggage in.)

frequency: <ruby>入<rt>い</rt></ruby>れる is extremely common across all registers — daily speech, cooking contexts, business, and set phrases like スイッチを<ruby>入<rt>い</rt></ruby>れる and お<ruby>茶<rt>ちゃ</rt></ruby>を<ruby>入<rt>い</rt></ruby>れる. One of the most useful high-frequency verbs at N4–N3 level.
anki_hint: <ruby>入<rt>い</rt></ruby>れる is the 'put-IN' transitive verb — if you can ask 'what did you put in?', this is your word; if the subject is the one going in, switch to <ruby>入<rt>はい</rt></ruby>る.
